title: Comparative Silane Surface Functionalization Strategies for Enhanced Bloch Surface Wave Biosensing of Anti-SARS-CoV-2 Antibodies
authors: Agostino Occhicone, Alberto Sinibaldi, Paola Di Matteo, Daniele Chiappetta, Riccardo Guadagnoli, Peter Munzert, Francesco Michelotti
abstract: Surface functionalization plays a decisive role in the performance of biosensors, as it governs the efficiency and stability of biomolecule immobilization at the sensor interface and, consequently, the overall performance of the biosensing platforms. In this work, we present a comparative study of three organosilane chemistries - APTES, APDMS, and CPTES - applied to a SiO2 terminated 1D photonic crystal able to sustain Bloch surface waves and designed to operate as optical biosensors in both label free and fluorescence enhanced modes. Each chemistry was evaluated through a standardized label-free protocol based on the interaction between immobilized SARS CoV 2 spike protein and its corresponding antibodies, enabling quantitative assessment of binding efficiency, nonspecific adsorption, and signal repeatability. CPTES exhibited the most favorable balance between specific signals, reduced variability, and low nonspecific adsorption. The three chemistries were subsequently tested in fluorescence mode for the detection of anti SARS CoV 2 IgG antibodies in human serum, demonstrating the suitability of BSW enhanced fluorescence for rapid serological analysis. Overall, the study identifies CPTES as the most robust and reproducible functionalization strategy among the three investigated for BSW biosensing and highlights the potential of the platform for fast, sensitive detection of clinically relevant antibodies.
summary: This paper compares three different silane-based surface functionalization strategies (APTES, APDMS, and CPTES) for a Bloch Surface Wave biosensor. The goal is to optimize the immobilization of SARS-CoV-2 spike proteins to detect antibodies more effectively. The researchers found that CPTES provides the most stable and specific signal with the lowest non-specific adsorption. The platform was successfully tested for rapid serological analysis using both label-free and fluorescence-enhanced methods in human serum.
main_problem: Optimizing the surface functionalization of a Bloch Surface Wave (BSW) biosensor to improve the efficiency, stability, and specificity of anti-SARS-CoV-2 antibody detection.
main_result: CPTES was identified as the most robust and reproducible silanization chemistry, providing the best balance of specific signal, low non-specific adsorption, and reduced variability for both label-free and fluorescence-enhanced modes.
method: A comparative study of three organosilane chemistries (APTES, APDMS, and CPTES) applied to a 1D photonic crystal, evaluated using label-free angular shift measurements and fluorescence-enhanced detection with quantum dots.
model_or_system: A 1D photonic crystal (1DPC) transducer consisting of alternating layers of SiO2 and Ta2O5, terminated with TiO2 and SiO2, operating in a Kretschmann-Raether configuration to sustain Bloch Surface Waves.
key_observables: Angular shifts (delta theta), differential angular shifts (delta theta DIFF), fluorescence emission intensity, and a variability metric (chi).
important_parameters: Wavelengths of 670 nm (label-free) and 405 nm (fluorescence); penetration depths of 125 nm and 53 nm; flow rate of 1.37 uL/s.
main_assumptions: The study assumes that the performance of the biosensor can be quantitatively assessed through standardized protein binding protocols and that the chemical stability of the silane layers is sufficient for clinical-style testing.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the silanization procedure and chemical structures; Figure 5 shows statistical boxplots and KDE curves; Figure 9 displays fluorescence maps and intensity plots; Figure 12 illustrates the optical readout system setup.
paper_structure: The paper begins with an introduction to surface functionalization in biosensing, followed by a detailed description of the 1DPC platform and the three silanization chemistries. It then presents the experimental protocols for label-free and fluorescence-enhanced modes, followed by a comparative analysis of the chemical performance, and concludes with the validation of the platform using human serum samples.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic representation of the silanization process. The procedure comprises four main steps, which vary depending on the chemistry. (I) Surface cleaning with piranha solution increases the density of surface —OH groups. (II) During SAM formation, the chip is immersed in solutions of APTES, APDMS, or CPTES; simplified molecular structure is shown. (III) In the curing step, the substrate is heated on a hot plate at 50–60 °C for 1 h. (IV) The activation step involves crosslinking with GAH for APTES and with CDI for APDMS; this step is not required for CPTES. After functionalization, substrates are stored in a vacuum chamber for at least 12 h before performing the assay.

Figure 2. (a) Sketch of a microscope slide with protein solutions during incubation. (b-d) CCD camera images acquired in LF mode at the beginning of the assays for APTES, APDMS and CPTES, respectively. Overlaid to the images is the spot mask used to identify the different regions on the 1DPC surface from Sig1 to Sig7 (dashed lines). Superimposed to the image (b), we plot the average reflectance profile in the seven different regions. The dip witnesses the excitation of the BSW resonance.

Figure 3. LF sensograms (𝛥𝜃 vs time) obtained using the LF-Protocol for the three surface chemistries: (a) APTES, (b) APDMS, and (c) CPTES. The three different sequential injections are highlighted with three different colours: the DI-H2O (grey), the BSA diluted in PBS 1× at 1 mg/mL (green) and Anti-S 1:250 diluted solution (blue).

Figure 9(a,b) present the background subtracted FLR emission maps acquired by the CCD at the conclusion of the assays, following QD incubation and rinsing with running buffer. The maps compare assays performed using Pos, Figure 9(a), and Neg, Figure 9b, human sera. The FLR maps were generated by resonantly exciting the BSW at 𝜆𝑒𝑥𝑐 and detecting the BSW-coupled fluorescence emission at 𝜆𝑒𝑚. Across all three functionalization chemistries, the FLR signal displays a spatial modulation that correlates precisely with the surface patterning, thereby validating the biochip's selectivity for anti-SARS-CoV-2 antibodies.
